address: Si Sanphet Road, Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya

description.en:
## Overview

Wat Phra Si Sanphet is the most important temple in Ayutthaya, equivalent to what the Temple of the Emerald Buddha is to Bangkok. Its three bell-shaped chedis, standing in a row against the sky, have become the defining symbol of Ayutthaya and appear on countless postcards and travel guides. Located on the grounds of the former Royal Palace, this was the personal temple of the Ayutthaya kings — no monks ever lived here, making it exclusively a royal chapel.

## History

The temple's origins may date to 1448 when King Boromatrailokanat built it within the palace grounds. The three signature chedis were constructed between 1448 and 1530 to enshrine the ashes of three Ayutthaya kings: Boromatrailokanat, Boromaracha III, and Ramathibodi II. The temple's namesake was a massive 16-meter standing Buddha image cast in bronze and covered with over 150 kilograms (330 pounds) of gold leaf. When the Burmese sacked the city in 1767, they smashed the statue and melted down the gold. King Rama I later collected the remaining bronze fragments and placed them in a chedi at Wat Pho in Bangkok. In 1991, the temple was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site along with the rest of Ayutthaya Historical Park.

## What to See

The **three iconic chedis** are the centerpiece — each stands roughly 16 meters wide at the base and features the distinctive bell shape of Ayutthaya-period architecture. Walk between them to appreciate their scale. Behind the chedis, explore the **remains of the old Royal Palace** foundations, including the site of the Sanphet Prasat Throne Hall. A replica of this throne hall was built at the Ancient City (Muang Boran) near Bangkok. The surrounding grounds feature additional smaller chedis and remnants of the monastery buildings.

## Practical Info

- **Entry fee:** 50 THB for foreigners, 10 THB for Thai nationals
- **Opening hours:** 08:00-18:00 daily
- **How to get there:** Central location within Ayutthaya Historical Park, adjacent to Wihan Phra Mongkhon Bophit; easily walkable from Wat Mahathat (10-minute walk)
- **Visit duration:** 45 minutes to 1 hour
- **Best season:** November to February (cool season) for comfortable exploration

best_time_to_visit: Early morning when the three chedis cast long shadows and tourist buses have not yet arrived
tips: Combine with the adjacent Wihan Phra Mongkhon Bophit, which houses a large bronze seated Buddha — it is free to enter
The three chedis photograph best from the eastern approach where you can capture all three in a single frame
Visit during cool season (November-February) as the open grounds offer little shade
